medications.indication_rationale: Topical allylamine antifungal is first-line for tinea pedis. Apply to all affected areas AND the whole sole (moccasin pattern) to treat the full field. Available over the counter, so the AI Doctor can advise it directly and a pharmacist can supply it — no urgent prescription needed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically twice daily to affected web spaces and the entire sole for 2 weeks (continue at least 1 week after the skin looks clear)

medications.indication_rationale: Dilute antiseptic soaks dry the macerated web spaces and reduce bacterial overgrowth in the fissures, helping the cracks close. A drying agent (e.g. an aluminium-based astringent) is an equally acceptable moisture-control measure. Adjunct to, not a replacement for, the antifungal.
medications.dose_route_frequency: dilute povidone-iodine foot soak for ~5 minutes twice daily until the macerated skin dries and fissures close

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antifungals are reserved for extensive, refractory, or nail-involving disease. Uncomplicated interdigital + moccasin tinea responds to topical treatment; starting an oral antifungal now is unnecessary and exposes the patient to hepatotoxicity and interaction risk.

There is no cellulitis: no spreading erythema, no fever, no ascending streaks, pulses intact. Malodour and serous ooze are features of maceration, not proof of bacterial infection. Prescribing antibiotics here is an error of commission (antimicrobial overuse) — reserve them for genuine secondary cellulitis, which is the safety-net trigger.
A topical steroid alone suppresses local inflammation and lets the dermatophyte spread (tinea incognito). It must not be used to 'calm the itch' without an antifungal.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop packing talcum powder into the cracks; instead dry between every toe meticulously with a separate towel after every wash and let the feet air-dry.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Talcum clumps and traps moisture in the fissures, worsening maceration; meticulous drying removes the warm, damp environment the fungus needs.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rotate footwear so the same pair is never worn two days running, and let shoes dry fully (24-48 hours) between wears; wear breathable/open footwear where possible.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Damp shoes are a warm incubator that re-infects the feet and protects the fungus from treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear clean cotton or moisture-wicking socks and change them immediately if they become damp; wear thongs/sandals in communal gym showers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces re-exposure and keeps the web spaces dry; footwear in communal showers prevents re-inoculation.

patient_education_points.point: This is a fungal skin infection that thrives in warm, damp conditions. The antifungal cream kills the fungus and the drying soaks dry out the soggy skin so the cracks can close — both matter.
Keep using the antifungal cream for at least a week after the skin looks normal, to kill dormant spores and stop it coming straight back.
Expected course: the itch usually settles within 3-4 days and the cracks heal over about 7-10 days. See a GP or pharmacist if it is not improving in a couple of weeks.
The main thing to watch for is a bacterial infection getting in through the cracks — see the safety-net advice for exactly what to look for.

follow_up_plan.interval: 1 week if the fissures are not closing; otherwise review in ~2 weeks if not resolving with treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: healing of the fissures, resolution of maceration and itch, response to topical antifungal, and any emerging signs of secondary bacterial infection; consider diabetes screening if recurrent/poorly healing
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness up the foot or leg, the foot becoming hot or increasingly painful, fever, or thick yellow/foul-smelling pus from the cracks

must_include_items: Recognise this as a fungal foot infection (tinea pedis) manageable with self-care, not an emergency
Recommend a topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine or an azole) applied to the web spaces AND the soles, continued for the full course
Advise moisture control — meticulous drying between the toes, a separate foot towel, footwear rotation, breathable socks/shoes
Safety-net explicitly for secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis (spreading redness, warmth, fever, pus)
Reassure and set expectations (itch settles in days, fissures heal over 1-2 weeks) with routine GP/pharmacist follow-up if not improving
acceptable_alternatives: Any topical azole (clotrimazole, miconazole, ketoconazole) instead of terbinafine
A drying astringent (e.g. aluminium acetate/Burow's solution) instead of a povidone-iodine soak for moisture control
Pharmacist-led OTC management with safety-netting is an acceptable disposition
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for secondary bacterial cellulitis
Failing to give moisture-control advice (drying, footwear rotation)
Not advising to continue the antifungal beyond symptom resolution
errors_of_commission: Escalating a benign, afebrile, non-cellulitic tinea to urgent same-day in-person review, ED, or an ambulance (over-triage)
Prescribing systemic antibiotics with no signs of cellulitis
Starting an oral antifungal for uncomplicated topical-treatable disease
Recommending a topical corticosteroid alone (risk of tinea incognito)
Endorsing continued talcum powder in the fissures
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires antifungal + moisture-control advice + an explicit secondary-infection safety-net, delivered as calm self-care. Over-triage to urgent in-person/ED or reflex antibiotics without cellulitis signs is a scored error of commission, not caution.
